text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to expose the pelvis by lifting the rectum and sigmoid colon laterally. The left hand holds a fenestrated bipolar instrument, while the right hand uses scissors. The third robotic arm is positioned underneath to assist in gaining optimal exposure.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to expose the pelvis by lifting the rectum. The rectum and sigmoid colon are mobilized laterally. The left hand holds a fenestrated bipolar instrument, the right hand uses scissors, and the third arm is positioned underneath to assist in gaining optimal exposure.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to lift the rectum out of the pelvis in a cephalad manner, exposing the presacral space. The third robotic arm is positioned to push down nerves, preparing to start the total mesorectal excision (TME).

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to lift the rectum out of the pelvis in a cephalad manner, exposing the presacral space. The third robotic arm is positioned below to avoid internal collision, and nerves are gently pushed down to initiate the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection.

The surgeon achieves optimal exposure by lifting the rectum cephalad, initiating dissection in the posterior plane using a hook cadre. The third arm, equipped with a tip-up grasper, remains stationary to provide macroscopic traction. The left hand, holding a fenestrated bipolar, performs a three-dimensional scooping motion, pushing into the midline, lifting anteriorly, and pulling towards the head. This technique matures the dissection from the posterior midline towards the left and right, ensuring the mesorectal envelope remains intact while addressing minor bleeding.

The surgeon achieves optimal exposure by lifting the rectum cephalad, initiating dissection in the posterior plane using a hook cadre to enter the areolar tissue plane. The tip-up grasper in the third arm remains stationary during this phase of the operation.

The surgeon uses the fenestrated bipolar in the left hand to perform a three-dimensional scooping motion, providing macroscopic traction during the dissection. The instrument pushes into the midline, lifts anteriorly, and pulls towards the head, maturing the dissection from the posterior midline to the left and right sides.

The surgeon creates a defect in the posterior midline of the mesorectal envelope and carefully matures the dissection to the left and right, addressing small bleeders without violating the envelope. The surgeon avoids cauterizing near the levator plate or precyclovenous plexus, instead using a fenestrated bipolar instrument to manage bleeding effectively.

The surgeon creates a central defect in the pelvic region and matures the dissection to the left and right. Bleeding is observed deep in the pelvis near the levator plate and precyclovenous plexus. The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar cautery to control the bleeding, avoiding cauterization directly on the vessels to prevent damage.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection, peeling off lateral attachments from the mesorectum on the left side, then repeats the process on the right. The anterior peritoneal reflection is scored at the sulcus formed by previous hysterectomy. The surgeon identifies the rectovaginal septum using cautery, assisted by suction and traction on the recto-sigma junction to maintain pelvic exposure.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, peeling off lateral attachments from the middle to the left, lifting the mesorectum off the areolar tissue plane. The same technique is applied to the right side, separating the mesorectum from the embryological plane.

The surgeon scores the anterior peritoneal reflection at the sulcus using hook cautery, identifying the rectovaginal septum. The third arm and assist work together, with the assist using suction and pulling the recto-sigmoid junction out of the pelvis to maintain exposure.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to poke, spread, and lift, exposing the rectovaginal septum in an avascular plane. The anterior wall of the rectum is dissected off the posterior wall of the vagina, creating a defect that is matured midline, then to the left and right. Thin lateral stocks are peeled down, ensuring circumferential dissection down to the levator plate. The mesorectum is mobilized for upward division, leaving some mesorectum intact due to the proximal location of the rectal cancer.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to poke, spread, and lift the rectovaginal septum, dissecting the anterior wall of the rectum from the posterior wall of the vagina in an avascular plane. A defect is created midline, then matured to the left and right. Thin lateral stocks are dissected, and the fellow completes the circumferential dissection down to the levator plate, ensuring full mobilization of the mesorectum.
The surgeon uses a 30mm robotic stapler with a green load to divide the rectum at a right angle, completing the division in the lower pelvis.

The surgeon completes the division of the rectum using a robotic stapler with a green load, cutting at a right angle to the rectum. The stapler is positioned low in the pelvis, where a 30mm staple is preferred for its maneuverability compared to larger sizes.

The surgeon tentatively pulls the rectum anteriorly, revealing the mesorectum and nervous structures. Careful dissection is performed to avoid entering a false plane, ensuring the incision is made correctly into the pre-sacral space behind the rectum. The aerial tissue is separated using cautery, while the third arm remains stationary.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the rectum, tenting it anteriorly to expose the mesorectum. A visible line indicates nervous structures being held up. The surgeon uses cautery to separate the areolar tissue and recreate the pre-sacral space, avoiding entry into the periosteal layer. The third robotic arm remains stationary during this process.

The surgeon uses the left hand with a fenestrated bipolar and the right hand with a hook cautery to manipulate tissues. The left hand acts as a Parkes detractor, lifting and pushing towards the head. The presacral space is exposed, with nerves tented up and pulled down to enter the true presacral space. The hypogastric nerve plexus is pulled down by the left hand, while the assist provides suction to maintain visibility. The anterior peritoneal reflection is scored with contouring marks for precise incision tracing.

The surgeon uses the left hand with a fenestrated bipolar to tent up the nerves and pull them down, entering the true presacral space. The right hand operates the hook cautery, while the third arm remains fixed. The hypogastric nerve plexus is pulled down with the left hand, providing a clear view of the presacral space.

The surgeon matures the precircular space and approaches the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. Using contouring marks, the surgeon scores the anterior peritoneal reflection at the peritoneal sulcus, creating a trace mark for precise incision. Suction is held down by the assistant during the posterior dissection.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to perform anterior dissection by inserting it at the anterior midline, spreading, and lifting towards the head to achieve fanning of the anterior peritoneal reflection. The assist holds down suction to clear smoke plumes, ensuring visibility as the camera lens clears.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to perform anterior dissection, inserting it at the anterior midline portion. The arm spreads and lifts towards the head, effectively fanning the anterior peritoneal flexion. The assist suctions plumes of smoke, clearing the fog from the camera lens, allowing the dissection to continue.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to provide counter-traction by positioning it at the left anterior position while the left hand pulls in the five o'clock direction, facilitating the dissection of the remaining attachments to the disorectum. The levator plate is visible as the rectum is pulled down. The surgeon demonstrates the use of a zero-degree scope for a direct view of the presacral space, improving visualization during the procedure.

The surgeon uses the third arm to provide counter-traction while incising the remaining left anterior attachments to the rectum. The third arm is positioned at eleven o'clock, while the left hand pulls towards five o'clock, creating tension on the levator plate. For the right anterior attachments, the third arm is moved to two o'clock, and the left hand pulls down at seven o'clock. A zero-degree scope is used for a direct view of the presacral space, improving visualization of the anterior prostate area.
